original_reasoning: Arabic اصطبل < Latin stabulum; stabilito is grammatical form of stabilio — same loanword family
Arabic جرَّة=earthenware/ceramic jar (جِرار=jars); Latin jarra=liquid measure based on jar size — documented loanword: Arabic جرة → Spanish/Latin jarra/jar
Arabic مرارة=bitterness (مرّ=bitter); Latin amarior=more bitter (comparative of amarus) — direct semantic match on bitterness
Arabic نَاطَهُ = to hang/suspend; anathema (from Greek ἀνάθημα) = thing hung up as votive offering — direct match on suspension/hanging as offering
ستيك: phonetic connection to stacta = oil of myrrh — possible Arabic-Latin contact form
ينّة = proper name; hyaena = hyena — contextual connection

Arabic سلق (root s-l-q) has multiple meanings: (1) boil/cook vegetables, (2) سِلْق = chard/beet plant (Beta vulgaris), (3) to ascend/climb. Latin siliqua = pod, husk, legume pod. Both refer specifically to edible plant parts. The phonetic correspondence s-l-q → silik- is near-perfect with consonant skeleton preservation. Chard (سلق) and pod-bearing plants (siliqua) share the edible vegetable domain. Strong candidate for shared Semitic agricultural vocabulary that entered Latin via Mediterranean trade.
Ar nafsh=to tease apart fibers with fingers/sheep grazing at night; Lat lanificus=working in wool (spinning/weaving); shared fiber-processing domain
